title: Spondyloarthropathies: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of Spondyloarthropathies
Apply clinical reasoning to Spondyloarthropathies management

sections.title: Overview of Spondyloarthropathies
sections.content:
**Spondyloarthropathies**

HLA-B27 association, ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, sacroiliitis

**Key Points:**
• HLA-B27 associated: AS, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis
• Ankylosing spondylitis: young male, inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30min, improves with exercise), sacroiliitis → bamboo spine
• Reactive arthritis: 'can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree' (conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis) post-infection

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• HLA-B27 associated: AS, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis
• Ankylosing spondylitis: young male, inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30min, improves with exercise), sacroiliitis → bamboo spine

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Reactive arthritis: 'can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree' (conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis) post-infection
• Psoriatic arthritis: DIP involvement (distinguishes from RA), dactylitis, enthesitis, pencil-in-cup deformity
• Treatment: NSAIDs first-line; TNF inhibitors for refractory disease

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Spondyloarthropathies:**

• Reactive arthritis: 'can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree' (conjunctivitis, urethritis, arthritis) post-infection
• Psoriatic arthritis: DIP involvement (distinguishes from RA), dactylitis, enthesitis, pencil-in-cup deformity
• Treatment: NSAIDs first-line; TNF inhibitors for refractory disease

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Spondyloarthropathies?

options.text: HLA-B27 associated: AS, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis
options.explanation: HLA-B27 associated: AS, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, IBD-associated arthritis

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Spondyloarthropathies?

options.text: Treatment: NSAIDs first-line; TNF inhibitors for refractory disease
options.explanation: Treatment: NSAIDs first-line; TNF inhibitors for refractory disease

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Spondyloarthropathies. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Ankylosing spondylitis: young male, inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30min, improves with exercise), sacroiliitis → bamboo spine
options.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis: young male, inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness >30min, improves with exercise), sacroiliitis → bamboo spine

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.
